---
title: "Transient Conduction"
---

$$
\newcommand{\Bi}{\mathrm{Bi}}
\newcommand{\Fo}{\mathrm{Fo}}
\newcommand{\dd}[2]{\dfrac{d#1}{d#2}}
\newcommand{\pp}[2]{\dfrac{\partial #1}{\partial #2}}
$$

## The transient heat conduction problem {#sec-transient-setup}

A solid body initially at a uniform temperature $T_i$ is suddenly exposed to a
fluid at temperature $T_\infty$ with convection coefficient $h$. We want to know
how the temperature inside the body evolves in time.

The governing PDE (for a 1-D geometry) is

$$
\pp{T}{t} = \alpha \, \nabla^2 T,
$$ {#eq-heat-pde}

subject to:

- **Initial condition:** $T(x,0) = T_i$
- **Boundary condition (convection):** $-k \pp{T}{n}\Big|_\text{surface} = h\bigl(T_s - T_\infty\bigr)$

### Dimensionless form

It is convenient to define a dimensionless excess temperature:

::: {.callout-note icon=false}
## Definition 1.1 — Dimensionless temperature
$$
\theta^*(r^*, \Fo) = \frac{T(r,t) - T_\infty}{T_i - T_\infty},
$$
where $r^* = r/L_c$ is the dimensionless spatial coordinate and $\Fo = \alpha t / L_c^2$ is the **Fourier number**.
:::

Note that $\theta^* = 1$ initially and $\theta^* \to 0$ as $t \to \infty$.

The Biot number characterises the ratio of internal conduction resistance to
external convection resistance:

::: {.callout-note icon=false}
## Definition 1.2 — Biot number
$$
\Bi = \frac{h L_c}{k},
$$
where $L_c$ is a characteristic length and $k$ is the thermal conductivity of the solid.
:::

---

## The lumped capacitance model ($\Bi \ll 1$) {#sec-lumped}

When $\Bi < 0.1$, spatial temperature gradients inside the body are small enough
to neglect. The entire solid is treated as a single lump at a single temperature.

::: {.callout-note icon=false}
## Theorem 1.1 — Lumped capacitance solution
For $\Bi \ll 1$, the temperature history is

$$
\theta^*_\text{lump}(\Fo) = e^{-\Bi \cdot \Fo}.
$$ {#eq-lumped}
:::

::: {.callout-tip collapse=true}
## Derivation
An energy balance on the entire body gives

$$
\rho V c_p \dd{T}{t} = -h A_s (T - T_\infty).
$$

Separating variables and integrating with $T(0)=T_i$:

$$
\ln\frac{T-T_\infty}{T_i-T_\infty} = -\frac{hA_s}{\rho V c_p}\,t.
$$

Identifying $\text{Bi} = hL_c/k$, $\text{Fo} = \alpha t/L_c^2$, and $L_c = V/A_s$
(for a plane wall), this becomes @eq-lumped.
:::

::: {.callout-caution}
## When is lumped analysis valid?
Use $\Bi < 0.1$ as the rule of thumb. For a sphere, $L_c = r_o / 3$, so
$\Bi = h r_o / (3k)$.
:::

**Example 1.1.** A copper sphere ($k = 401\ \text{W/m·K}$, $\rho = 8933\ \text{kg/m}^3$,
$c_p = 385\ \text{J/kg·K}$) of radius $r_o = 5\ \text{mm}$ is suddenly placed in
an airstream at $T_\infty = 20\ \text{°C}$ with $h = 100\ \text{W/m}^2\text{·K}$.
The initial temperature is $T_i = 200\ \text{°C}$. Calculate the Biot number
and determine whether the lumped model applies.

```{ojs}
//| echo: true
//| code-fold: true

ex1 = {
  const k = 401, r_o = 5e-3, h = 100
  const Lc = r_o / 3
  const Bi = h * Lc / k
  return { Lc, Bi, valid: Bi < 0.1 }
}

html`<pre>Characteristic length  Lc = ${(ex1.Lc*1e3).toFixed(3)} mm
Biot number            Bi = ${ex1.Bi.toFixed(5)}
Lumped model valid?       ${ex1.valid ? "Yes (Bi < 0.1)" : "No  (Bi >= 0.1)"}</pre>`
```

---

## One-dimensional solutions {#sec-1d}

For geometries where spatial variation matters ($\Bi \ge 0.1$), we solve the
heat equation with separation of variables, yielding an infinite series.

### The one-term approximation {#sec-oneterm}

For $\Fo \ge 0.2$, the series converges rapidly and only the **first term** is
needed.

::: {.callout-note icon=false}
## Theorem 1.2 — One-term approximation (sphere)
For a sphere of radius $r_o$, the dimensionless centre temperature is

$$
\theta^*(0, \Fo) \approx C_1 \, e^{-\zeta_1^2 \Fo},
$$

and at any radial location $r^* = r/r_o$:

$$
\theta^*(r^*, \Fo) \approx C_1 \, e^{-\zeta_1^2 \Fo} \cdot \frac{\sin(\zeta_1 r^*)}{\zeta_1 r^*},
$$ {#eq-oneterm}

where $\zeta_1$ is the first root of the transcendental equation

$$
1 - \zeta_1 \cot(\zeta_1) = \Bi,
$$

and $C_1 = 4\left[\sin(\zeta_1) - \zeta_1\cos(\zeta_1)\right] / \left[2\zeta_1 - \sin(2\zeta_1)\right]$.
:::

**Example 1.2.** Find $\zeta_1$ and $C_1$ for $\Bi = 1$.

```{ojs}
//| echo: true
//| code-fold: true

ex2 = {
  const Bi = 1.0
  // brentq is defined in sphere_demo.qmd — but we need it here too,
  // so inline a simple bisection for this standalone cell
  function bisect(f, a, b, tol=1e-10) {
    for (let i = 0; i < 100; i++) {
      const m = (a + b) / 2
      if (Math.abs(b - a) < tol) return m
      f(a) * f(m) < 0 ? (b = m) : (a = m)
    }
    return (a + b) / 2
  }
  const f = z => 1 - z / Math.tan(z) - Bi
  const zeta1 = bisect(f, 1e-6, Math.PI - 1e-6)
  const C1 = 4*(Math.sin(zeta1) - zeta1*Math.cos(zeta1))
           / (2*zeta1 - Math.sin(2*zeta1))
  return { Bi, zeta1, C1 }
}

html`<pre>Bi   = ${ex2.Bi.toFixed(1)}
ζ₁   = ${ex2.zeta1.toFixed(6)} rad
C₁   = ${ex2.C1.toFixed(6)}</pre>`
```

### Full Fourier series {#sec-series}

The exact solution for a sphere is:

::: {.callout-note icon=false}
## Theorem 1.3 — Fourier series solution (sphere)
$$
\theta^*(r^*, \Fo) = \sum_{n=1}^{\infty} C_n \, e^{-\zeta_n^2 \Fo}
  \cdot \frac{\sin(\zeta_n r^*)}{\zeta_n r^*},
$$ {#eq-series}

where $\zeta_n$ are successive positive roots of $1 - \zeta\cot(\zeta) = \Bi$
and the coefficients are

$$
C_n = \frac{4[\sin(\zeta_n) - \zeta_n\cos(\zeta_n)]}{2\zeta_n - \sin(2\zeta_n)}.
$$
:::

At the centre ($r^* \to 0$) we use L'Hôpital's rule:
$\sin(\zeta_n r^*)/(\zeta_n r^*) \to 1$.

::: {.callout-note}
The one-term approximation (@eq-oneterm) uses only the $n=1$ term of
@eq-series. For $\Fo \ge 0.2$, higher terms decay exponentially fast and
the error is typically less than 2 %.
:::

---

## Summary table {#sec-summary}

| Model | When to use | Temperature profile |
|-------|-------------|---------------------|
| Lumped ($\Bi < 0.1$) | Uniform internal $T$ | $\theta^* = e^{-\Bi\,\Fo}$ |
| One-term ($\Fo \ge 0.2$) | Moderate $\Bi$, large $\Fo$ | @eq-oneterm |
| Full series | Any $\Bi$, any $\Fo > 0$ | @eq-series |

: Transient conduction models for a sphere. {#tbl-summary}
